title: Teething trouble in babies
excerpt: When the first tooth pushes through, babies get fussy, drool, and sleep badly. Ayurveda calls this Dantodbheda and treats it as a natural passage, not a disease, with gentle soothing care.
at_a_glance: Teething (Dantodbheda) is a normal growth stage, not a disease, and classical pediatric texts treat it as such.
Soothe with safe gum pressure, frequent gentle feeds, drool-free skin, and lots of calm cuddling.
Fever, refusal of all feeds, repeated vomiting, bloody or frequent loose motions, or dehydration signs mean see a doctor today.

body.heading: what is happening when your baby is teething
paragraphs: Somewhere around six or seven months, your baby starts chewing on everything, drools onto every shirt, and turns cranky for no reason you can see. The gums look swollen and a little red. This is teething, and almost every parent goes through these restless weeks.

Ayurveda calls the eruption of the milk teeth Dantodbheda, which simply means the coming out of the teeth. The classical text of children's care, the Kashyapa Samhita, treats this as an ordinary stage of growth rather than an illness. It describes the period when teeth push through the gum as one that naturally brings discomfort, mild fever, loose motions, and fussiness in many infants (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana, on Dantodbheda).

The old texts noticed what parents still notice today: a baby who is otherwise healthy can run a low fever, drool heavily, refuse feeds, and cry more during these days. The gum is being stretched from inside, and the body responds. Sushruta, while describing the milk teeth and the structures of the mouth, also places tooth eruption within normal child development (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).
body.pullQuote: a sick baby is not just a teething baby, and it is easy to blame teething for something more serious

body.heading: the Ayurvedic view of the fuss and fever
paragraphs: Ayurveda does not see teething as one single problem but as a brief upset in the baby's still-forming system. The texts on children's care explain that an infant's body is tender and its agni, the digestive strength, is delicate, so small disturbances show up easily as loose stools or a mild rise in temperature (Kashyapa Samhita, Khilasthana, on the care of the child).

Because of this, Kashyapa repeatedly stresses gentleness over strong medicine for the very young. The guiding instinct is to soothe, to keep the baby calm and fed, and to let the tooth come through, rather than to suppress every symptom. The discomfort passes on its own once the tooth breaks the surface.

This is also why mild, watery loose motions during teething are watched but not panicked over in the classical approach, as long as the baby stays alert, takes feeds, and passes urine normally. The concern was always the baby's overall strength, not the single symptom.

body.heading: gentle ways to soothe a teething baby
paragraphs: The most useful thing you can offer a teething baby is steady comfort and something safe to press the gums against. Counter-pressure on the swollen gum genuinely eases the ache, which is why babies chew so much during these weeks.

Keep feeds going. A teething baby may nurse or take milk in shorter, fussier bouts, so offer the breast or feed more often rather than forcing a full feed. Wipe the drool gently and often so the skin around the mouth and chin does not get sore and rashy. Extra cuddling, rocking, and a calm room help more than anything, because a settled baby feels the discomfort less.

Keep the routine soft and predictable: regular feeds, enough sleep, a warm and quiet space. Ayurveda's whole instinct for the infant is calm, warmth, and nourishment, and that instinct serves you well here.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: Teething itself is normal, but a sick baby is not just a teething baby, and it is easy to blame teething for something more serious. See a doctor or a qualified vaidya promptly if your baby has a high fever, is very drowsy or floppy, refuses all feeds, vomits repeatedly, or has watery loose motions that are frequent or contain blood. Signs of dehydration, such as far fewer wet nappies, a dry mouth, no tears when crying, or a sunken soft spot on the head, need same-day medical attention.

Any baby under three months with a fever, a fever that climbs or lasts more than a day or two, a rash, breathing trouble, or a baby who simply does not seem right deserves a doctor's eye without waiting. Trust your sense that something is off.

This article is general information drawn from classical Ayurvedic texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. For a fussy, feverish, or unwell infant, always have a paediatrician or qualified vaidya examine your child.

try_this_today.title: Safe gum pressure
try_this_today.description: Let your baby chew on a clean, firm teething ring or a clean finger. The counter-pressure on the swollen gum eases the ache, which is exactly why babies chew everything in sight during these weeks.

try_this_today.title: Feed little and often
try_this_today.description: A teething baby may take milk in shorter, fussier bouts. Offer the breast or feed more frequently rather than forcing a full feed, so nourishment stays steady through the cranky days.

try_this_today.title: Keep the chin dry
try_this_today.description: Wipe drool gently and often, and pat the skin around the mouth dry. This prevents the sore, rashy patches that heavy drooling causes around the lips and chin.

try_this_today.title: Lean on calm
try_this_today.description: Extra rocking, cuddling, and a quiet, warm room settle a teething baby more than anything. A calm baby feels the discomfort less, which is the whole instinct of Ayurvedic infant care.

words_to_know.definition: The eruption of the milk teeth, the coming out of a baby's first teeth.
The body's digestive fire or strength, considered delicate in an infant.
A qualified practitioner of Ayurvedic medicine.

sources: Kashyapa Samhita, on Dantodbheda (the eruption of the milk teeth) in the text's child-care teaching · Kashyapa Samhita, Khilasthana (sections on the care of the infant) · Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana (development of the child)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
